title: T-058 EACCES message regex tautologically matches the impl's hard-coded 'Failed to read' prefix
description: T-058 asserts the corrupt-EACCES message matches /read|permission|access/i. The implementation in state.ts unconditionally prefixes ALL non-ENOENT readFile errors with 'Failed to read state file: ...'. The literal substring 'read' inside that prefix satisfies the regex regardless of whether the impl actually distinguished EACCES from any other error code. An impl that classified all readFile errors uniformly (not branching on err.code) — i.e. exactly what the contract A6 'cycle-1 C002/C010 lesson' wants to prevent — would still pass this assertion solely on the literal prefix.
impact: The regression bar A6 cares about (treating ENOENT distinctly from EACCES/ENOTDIR with intelligible wording) is weaker than it appears. T-057 (ENOENT → absent) does enforce the branching, so the dimension is not unprotected, but the EACCES-specific message-quality check is effectively a no-op.
recommendation: Tighten T-058 to assert the message contains the actual error code or the synthetic word 'permission' / 'access' separately, OR exclude the literal prefix from the haystack before regex-matching.

title: T-077 / T-083 archive-path reference assertion is over-permissive (JSON.stringify haystack)
description: T-077 verifies the state-corrupt warning references the archive path by stringifying the entire warning object and checking the archivedTo string appears anywhere in the JSON. The mocked loadState supplies BOTH archivedTo AND a message containing archivedTo. Two structurally different impls pass: (a) carry archivedTo as a first-class field (contract intent), (b) drop the field but leave the path in the message string only. Neither does the inverse impl-defect (field present but mis-spelled message) get caught. Same shape applies to T-083's 'corrupt warning carries an archive reference' check.
impact: The contract A10 specifies the warning 'names the archive path' and the implementer chose to carry it as a typed field (good); a future refactor that drops the typed field while preserving the message would silently weaken consumers but pass the tests.
recommendation: Split the assertion: explicitly assert warning.archivedTo === archivedTo (typed field present) AND optionally that the message references it. Same for T-083.

title: T-061 verifies call ORDER but not that fsync targets the fd that wrote the tmp bytes
description: T-061 records invocationCallOrder for writeFile / fsync / rename and asserts writeFile < fsync < rename. The impl in state.ts calls `fsPromises.writeFile(tmpPath, ...)` (which opens its own internal fd, writes, closes), then opens a SEPARATE fd via `fs.openSync(tmpPath, 'r')` and fsyncs that read-only fd, then renames. Fsyncing a read-only fd that did not own the write is questionable — POSIX permits it but on some filesystems the durability guarantee depends on the writing fd being fsynced before close. The test passes despite this gap because it only correlates timestamps across spies, never verifying the fsync target matches the fd from the write.
impact: An impl that satisfies the call-order check but does not actually flush the buffer (e.g. fsyncs a stale fd) would still pass T-061. Contract A7 says 'fsync(fd) on the open tmp file' — the test cannot distinguish a meaningful fsync from a ceremonial one.
recommendation: Either (a) assert that the fsync spy's first argument is a numeric fd and that the same fd was returned by an open call observed before the fsync, or (b) augment the test to verify that on a synthetic crash (process abort between writeFile and fsync) the file is consistent. (b) is hard; (a) is feasible.

title: start.tool.test.ts mocks state.js wholesale — T-077/T-078/T-080/T-082/T-083/T-084 are mapping tests, not integration
description: Every cycle-2 assertion in start.tool.test.ts runs against `vi.mock('../mcp/server/src/state.js')` — loadState/saveState/STATE_SCHEMA_VERSION are stubs. This means tests T-077 (corrupt → warning + archive), T-078 (schema-mismatch → warning, no archive), T-082 (key set), T-083 (distinct kinds), and T-084 (message regexes) test only `runStart`'s mapping from a synthetic LoadStateResult onto the response — they do not exercise the real loadState, real archiving, or the real schema validator. Real integration is delegated to harness.mcp.test.ts T-089/T-090/T-091, which DO drive disk fixtures end-to-end. This is a deliberate split (and tests/state.test.ts handles state.ts in isolation), so the surface is covered, but a reviewer should not treat T-077..T-084 as proving the integration — they prove only the response-mapper.
impact: If the harness tests fail to actually round-trip through the real loadState (e.g. because the SDK shape changes silently), the start.tool.test.ts suite would still go green and no cycle-2 unit signal would catch the integration regression. T-089/T-090/T-091 are load-bearing.
recommendation: No code change needed; surface this in the cycle's review notes so that any future trimming of harness.mcp.test.ts is recognized as a coverage regression.

title: T-085 stub server only records tool names — registerTools = (s) => s.tool('start', ...) with a no-op handler would pass
description: T-085 instantiates a fake server whose `tool()` method just pushes the name into an array, then asserts `registered.includes('start')`. The handler argument and schema are discarded. Consequences: an impl that registers `start` with a handler that throws, returns the wrong shape, or ignores its arguments would pass T-085. Real handler exercise is via T-086/T-087 (real Client + InMemoryTransport), which IS the meaningful coverage. T-085 alone is essentially a name-presence assertion.
impact: Low — T-086/T-087 carry the actual integration weight. T-085 is a smoke test that the export exists and is callable, which is fine framing for the contract A12 'registers every cycle-1 + cycle-2 tool' requirement (it does prove the .tool() call site exists for `start`).
recommendation: Optional: extend T-085 to also assert the recorded entry has a function-typed handler argument (call[3] is a function) — would catch `s.tool('start')` with no handler. Not blocking.

title: T-067 does not verify the validator's `value` round-trips all input fields (only checks schema_version)
description: T-067 asserts `result.ok === true`, `result.value` is truthy, and `result.value.schema_version === 1`. The impl in schemas/state.ts reconstructs the value object by re-extracting fields (cursor is shallow-rebuilt with only phase_id/spot_id; ladder/history/personalization are typed-cast through). The test never verifies `value.cursor`, `value.ladder`, `value.history`, `value.personalization` against the input. An impl that returned `{ ok: true, value: { schema_version: 1 } as State }` (dropping every other field) would pass T-067. The integration is rescued by T-064 / T-094 round-tripping via JSON serialize, but those go through saveState→loadState, not validateState directly.
impact: validateState's contract per the contract.md schemas/state.ts entry is 'returns { ok: true, value: <input> }'. The current test does not enforce input → value field-fidelity at the validator boundary.
recommendation: Add `expect((result as any).value).toEqual(input)` to T-067, or extend it to assert each top-level field deep-equals the input.

title: T-089 only checks archive existence count, not byte-for-byte content (state.test.ts T-048 covers this separately)
description: T-089 asserts `archives.length > 0` for the E-006 corrupt scenario through the harness — sufficient to prove the archive file appears. It does not verify the archive bytes equal the original corrupt fixture bytes. T-048 in tests/state.test.ts does verify byte-for-byte equality, so the property is asserted somewhere. But the harness scenario (which is the cycle-2 E-006 evidence) does not by itself catch a regression where the archive write becomes 0-byte or content-truncated when called through the MCP transport seam (a transport that swallowed bytes mid-flight, for instance). Different code path than the unit suite.
impact: Low — T-048 catches archive content fidelity in the unit pass. Cycle 4+ readers of E-006 should not assume the harness scenario alone proves byte-fidelity.
recommendation: Optional: add an extra assert in T-089 that `fs.readFileSync(archives[0], 'utf8') === <corrupt fixture bytes>`. Costs one read and matches the unit-suite assertion strength.

title: T-088 source-grep guard misses dynamic-import / aliased-name evasions of `runStart`
description: T-088 reads scripts/e2e/harness.ts as text and applies three regex guards: no `import { runStart }` binding, no path containing `tools/start`, no `if (toolName === 'start')` branch. None of these catch (a) a dynamic `await import('../../mcp/server/src/tools/start.js')` whose result is destructured under a non-`runStart` name, or (b) an aliased import like `import { runStart as foo }` which does match the brace regex but only because `runStart` is the original token — fine; however, `import * as start from '../../mcp/server/src/tools/start.js'` would slip the second regex if the import path uses `tools/start.js` exactly... actually it would match. The dynamic-import path is the real evasion vector. Contract A13 wants 'adding a new tool to registerTools is sufficient'; the regex guards are a heuristic, not a proof.
impact: Low — the harness as written cleanly uses only registerTools + Client. A future contributor circumventing the guard via dynamic import would pass T-088 silently while violating A13.
recommendation: Optional: add a regex for `import\(['"][^'"]*tools/start` covering the dynamic-import form. Not blocking.
